editorialPlanDraft.answerThesis: There is no one Indian labour market: whether you work at all, what kind of work you get, whether you are paid for it, and what it pays are decided less by 'the economy' than by who you are (sex, schooling, caste, religion, marital status, age) and where you were born (state, village or city). The official unemployment rate of about 3% hides a deep structure of precarious work and forced labour.
requiredConcepts: LFPR (labour force participation rate) is the share of working-age people (15+) who are working OR looking for work; WPR is the share actually working; UR (unemployment rate) is the share of the LABOUR FORCE that wants work but cannot find it, not a share of the whole population. A low UR can coexist with a real jobs problem.

Usual status (PS+SS) is a person's main activity over the past year including subsidiary work; it is the lens used throughout this page. Counting subsidiary and unpaid family work is what lifts measured female participation, so 'a woman works' on this measure can mean unpaid help on the family farm, not a paid job of her own.

Informal employment means work with no written contract, no social security and no paid leave; it is the default Indian working condition and exists even INSIDE formal-looking salaried jobs, which is why a payslip is not the same as a 'good job'.
Self-employment in India is mostly survival, not entrepreneurship: an own-account farmer or a street vendor is 'self-employed', so the label flattens enormous differences in security and income.

The education-unemployment paradox: unemployment RISES with schooling because the unlettered cannot afford to wait and take any work going (so they count as employed), while the educated can lean on family and hold out for the scarce salaried jobs their degree was for; joblessness here is a privilege of having options, not proof that education fails.

NEET is the share of young people not in employment, education or training; female NEET is far higher than male mostly because unpaid domestic and care work is not counted as employment, so 'doing nothing' is a measurement artefact, not idleness.

Caste shows up less in the unemployment rate (everyone poor cannot afford to search) and more in the KIND of work: the most precarious casual day-labour falls disproportionately on Dalits and Adivasis, while secure salaried jobs and higher pay concentrate among upper-caste workers.

Female participation does not rise neatly with prosperity: some of the poorest, most agricultural states have the highest female participation (women working the land out of need), while rich, urban places can be among the lowest, and a wealthy, highly-educated state like Kerala sits only mid-table; read high participation as 'more women in some kind of work', not automatically 'a better deal for women'.

A 'secure formal job' in the compounding chart means a regular salaried job that carries social security; the chart shows the share of each stacked-identity profile that holds one, which is why it falls so steeply.

Household monthly consumption (MPCE) is used as a stand-in for living standards; informality is near-universal among the poorest households and still the majority even among the richest, so formality is a thin sliver at the top, not a middle-class norm.

This page is a single 2025 cross-section, so it shows the STRUCTURE of who-gets-what, not a trend over time; do not describe anything as rising or falling unless the evidence packet contains more than one year.

sectionPlan.heading: Why does whether you work at all first depend on your sex?
sectionPlan.job: Introduce the deepest fault line: nearly four in five men are in the labour force, but only two in five women. Explain LFPR and WPR, the measurement (usual status including subsidiary work), and that the gap is the starting point for every other divide.
conceptsToExplain: LFPR vs WPR
Usual status (PS+SS) and its effect on female measurement
The sex gap in participation is the first and largest split
sectionPlan.caveat: Female LFPR includes unpaid family workers; many women counted as 'working' are not earning their own income.

sectionPlan.heading: Why don't most Indians have a job, only work?
sectionPlan.job: Show the anatomy of work: self-employment dominates, followed by casual labour, with regular salaried jobs a minority. Explain that 'self-employed' includes survivalist own-account workers and unpaid family labour, and that 'job' implies a regular employer.
conceptsToExplain: Self-employment as survival vs. entrepreneurship
Casual labour as day-to-day insecurity
Regular salaried work is what is often meant by 'job'
sectionPlan.caveat: The data is a single snapshot; the share of regular salaried work is not changing over time here.

sectionPlan.heading: Why do nine in ten Indians work with no contract and no safety net?
sectionPlan.job: Introduce informality: the vast majority of workers lack a written contract, social security, or paid leave. Show that even among salaried workers, a large share is informal. Compare rural and urban informality.
conceptsToExplain: Informal employment definition (no contract, no social security, no paid leave)
Informality is the default, not an anomaly
sectionPlan.caveat: The headline 85% informality masks variation by caste, consumption, and location.

sectionPlan.heading: Why are the people with degrees the ones without work?
sectionPlan.job: Present the education-unemployment paradox: the unlettered have near-zero unemployment, while graduates and postgraduates face rates of 13% or higher. Explain the ability to wait, the mismatch between credentials and available salaried jobs.
conceptsToExplain: Unemployment rate measures those actively seeking work
The unlettered cannot afford to search; the educated can hold out
A higher unemployment rate can mean more options, not absolute deprivation
sectionPlan.caveat: This is structure, not trend; we are not showing how these rates have changed over time.

sectionPlan.heading: Why is a degree the riskiest qualification for the young?
sectionPlan.job: Show that the education paradox is sharpest among 15–29-year-olds: youth graduate unemployment reaches 25.6%, and for postgraduates 32.2%. Highlight the intense mismatch and the risk of prolonged job search.
conceptsToExplain: Youth unemployment is concentrated among the educated
The graduate unemployment rate is a measure of the formal-job scarcity
sectionPlan.caveat: A high rate for a small group can be noisy; nonetheless, the pattern is stark.

sectionPlan.heading: Why are almost a third of working women paid nothing?
sectionPlan.job: Show that women's work is far more likely to be unpaid family labour: 28.8% of working women are unpaid, vs 8.2% of men. This underscores that measured female participation includes a large share of invisible, unrecognised work.
conceptsToExplain: Unpaid family worker: helps on the family farm or enterprise without direct payment
The gap in unpaid work is a major component of the gender gap in earnings
Implications for women's bargaining power and economic independence
sectionPlan.caveat: Being an unpaid family worker does not necessarily mean no economic contribution; it means the survey does not capture direct payment.

sectionPlan.heading: How does marriage decide whether a woman works?
sectionPlan.job: Show that female LFPR varies dramatically by marital status: never-married women have the lowest rate (24.5%), married women higher (45.3%), and divorced/separated women the highest (64.6%). Explain the interplay of necessity and the division of domestic work.
conceptsToExplain: Never-married women often live in households where they are dependents or students
Married women's participation is pushed by economic need in poorer households
Divorced/separated women have the highest participation, likely out of necessity
The same pattern does not apply to men
sectionPlan.caveat: Causality is complex; lower participation for never-married women may also reflect age and education factors.

sectionPlan.heading: How does a woman's faith track whether she works?
sectionPlan.job: Show varying female LFPR by religion: Hindu (41.5%), Muslim (31.4%), Christian (46.7%), Sikh (33%), Buddhist (44.7%), Jain (27.4%). Note that these are correlates tangled with region, urbanisation, and household income.
conceptsToExplain: Religion is a marker of community norms, not direct causation
Lower Muslim female LFPR is partly explained by concentration in certain states and lower urbanisation
These patterns intersect with caste, education, and geography
sectionPlan.caveat: Do not imply causation; these are aggregated associations that hide sharp within-group variation.

sectionPlan.heading: Why does a woman's state decide her odds of working?
sectionPlan.job: Use the choropleth map to show the enormous spread in female LFPR across states: from 68.8% in Sikkim to 17.9% in Delhi. Explain that high participation often reflects agricultural necessity, not liberation; low participation can happen in wealthy, urbanised states.
conceptsToExplain: High female LFPR in many hill and tribal states is driven by subsistence agriculture and matrilineal traditions
Low female LFPR in states like Delhi, Punjab, Haryana reflects a combination of high incomes, lack of suitable paid work, and patriarchal norms
sectionPlan.caveat: A choropleth can mask within-state variation; state-level data can be noisy for smaller states.

sectionPlan.heading: Who gets handed the hardest, least secure work?
sectionPlan.job: Show the caste hierarchy in casual labour: ST (26.4%), SC (28.6%), OBC (20.2%), Others (12.6%). Casual labour is the most precarious form of work, paid daily with no security. Caste determines exposure.
conceptsToExplain: Casual labour is day-to-day manual work, often in agriculture, construction
The caste gradient in casual work mirrors historical exclusion from land and capital
sectionPlan.caveat: The casual category may not capture all forms of precarious work; informal self-employment can be just as insecure.

sectionPlan.heading: And who gets the secure salaried jobs?
sectionPlan.job: Show the mirror image: regular salaried work is highest for Others (33.1%), then OBC (23.7%), SC (22.8%), ST (14.1%). The secure 'job' is still a largely upper-caste privilege.
conceptsToExplain: Regular salaried work means a permanent position with an employer
Even this category includes many informal arrangements (see formality-detail chart)
The caste gradient in regular work narrows only slowly
sectionPlan.caveat: This is share among workers, not population shares; lower-caste workers are also fewer in absolute numbers.

sectionPlan.heading: What is the caste pay gap?
sectionPlan.job: Show median monthly salaried wages by caste: ST ₹13,000, SC ₹12,500, OBC ₹15,000, Others ₹18,000. Even among those who hold a salaried job, caste marks a clear pay hierarchy.
conceptsToExplain: These are medians for all salaried workers, not adjusted for education or occupation
The gap partly reflects occupational segregation and discrimination
The SC disadvantage is not just access to salaried work but pay within it
sectionPlan.caveat: Wage data for salaried workers is more reliable than for casual or self-employed; but caste categories are approximate in survey data.

sectionPlan.heading: Why is measured unemployment lowest for the most disadvantaged?
sectionPlan.job: Show that unemployment rates by caste are remarkably flat (ST 2.1%, SC 3.3%, OBC 3.2%, Others 3.6%). Explain that this reflects the inability of the poor to afford open unemployment: they must accept any work, so they are never 'looking for work'.
conceptsToExplain: Low unemployment does not mean good labour market outcomes
The concept of 'open unemployment' is biased toward those with resources to search
Forced self-employment and low hours are the hidden unemployment
sectionPlan.caveat: The slightly higher UR for Others could reflect a larger share of educated youth holding out.

sectionPlan.heading: Why is unemployment almost entirely a young person's condition?
sectionPlan.job: Show the age gradient: youth (15–29) UN rate 10.3%, prime age (30–59) 0.7%, older (60+) 0.1%. The young are the only ones who can remain jobless while searching; older workers cannot.
conceptsToExplain: Youth unemployment is driven by the entry of educated cohorts into a labour market with few salaried openings
Prime-age workers are either employed or out of the labour force
Age segmentation is sharp and structural
sectionPlan.caveat: The low prime-age rate does not imply secure work; most are in informal employment.

sectionPlan.heading: Where are all the young women who vanish from the data?
sectionPlan.job: Show the NEET rates: female NEET 44.3%, male 8.1%. The bulk of young women not in employment, education, or training are engaged in unpaid domestic work, an invisible contribution to the economy.
conceptsToExplain: NEET = not in employment, education or training
High female NEET is largely a measurement issue: domestic work is not counted as economic activity
The gender gap in NEET is a call to recognize the value of unpaid work
sectionPlan.caveat: NEET includes those who have given up looking for work, a discouraged worker phenomenon.

sectionPlan.heading: Why do you mostly work for yourself in the village?
sectionPlan.job: Show the rural work structure: self-employed 60.9%, casual 24.3%, regular salaried 14.8%. Agriculture and allied activities dominate, and the village economy is centred on own-account and family work.
conceptsToExplain: Rural self-employment is largely farming on small holdings
Regular salaried work in rural areas is often government jobs or plantation work
This structure shapes family work patterns and migration
sectionPlan.caveat: Rural classification is based on census definition; many peri-urban areas blur the line.

sectionPlan.heading: Why do you mostly work for a wage in the city?
sectionPlan.job: Show the urban work structure: self-employed 38.2%, regular salaried 49.7%, casual 12.1%. The city runs on wage work and small trade. The salaried job is an urban phenomenon.
conceptsToExplain: Urban regular salaried work includes both formal and informal jobs
Self-employment in cities is often petty trade and services
The urban-rural divide in work status is stark
sectionPlan.caveat: Urban casual labourers are often migrants in construction; their conditions can be more precarious than rural casual labour.

sectionPlan.heading: What do India's workers actually do all day?
sectionPlan.job: Show the industrial structure: agriculture 41.2%, trade/transport/services 23.1%, construction 13%, manufacturing 11.8%, public admin/edu/health 10%, mining/utilities 0.6%. Despite GDP growth, employment remains anchored in low-productivity agriculture.
conceptsToExplain: Employment structure lags output structure; agriculture employs 41% but produces a much smaller share of GDP
Manufacturing's small share is a classic 'jobless growth' symptom
Construction is a major absorber of male casual labour
sectionPlan.caveat: Industry classification is broad; within trade and services are both highly skilled and very low-end jobs.

sectionPlan.heading: Why isn't a salaried job always a 'good' job?
sectionPlan.job: Show that even among regular salaried workers, 55.8% have no written contract, 53.4% no social security, and 47.6% no paid leave. The 'good job' is not defined by having an employer but by having formal protections.
conceptsToExplain: A 'regular salaried' job can still be informal if it lacks contract, social security, or leave
The formality gap within salaried work shows the distance between aspiration and reality
Social security coverage is a key marker of a good job
sectionPlan.caveat: The three indicators may overlap; a worker could lack one but have another.

sectionPlan.heading: What does a month of work pay, and who gets which deal?
sectionPlan.job: Show pay divides: formal salaried median ₹25,456 vs informal salaried ₹12,000; men ₹15,500 vs women ₹10,000. The formal-informal gap and gender gap compound. Also show casual daily wage of ₹400 for context.
conceptsToExplain: Wages are medians for that group; many earn less
The formal premium is large and reflects both productivity and bargaining power
The gender pay gap is also large within salaried work
A casual labourer earning ₹400/day has no income on workless days
sectionPlan.caveat: Self-employed earnings are uneven and not shown here; salaried wages are better measured.

sectionPlan.heading: Why is the poorest households' work the most precarious?
sectionPlan.job: Show informality by consumption quartile: Q1 92.8%, Q2 89.2%, Q3 84%, Q4 71.4%. Even the richest quartile has 7 in 10 workers informal. The gradient is clear but informality is pervasive.
conceptsToExplain: Informality and poverty are mutually reinforcing
The link between household consumption and worker informality shows the structural trap
sectionPlan.caveat: Consumption quartiles are based on PLFS-reported MPCE, which may be noisy.

sectionPlan.heading: What happens when you stack the advantages?
sectionPlan.job: Show the compounding chart: from 38.9% of urban upper-caste male graduates holding a secure formal job, down to 0.3% of rural Dalit/Adivasi low-educated women. The intersection of caste, gender, location, and education creates a chasm.
conceptsToExplain: Secure formal job = regular salaried + social security
The steep decline shows that disadvantages interact, not just add
This single chart encapsulates the entire story of who works in India
sectionPlan.caveat: The profiles are illustrative; they are not the only possible combinations. The gradient is what matters.

chartPlan.title: Whether you work at all comes down, first, to your sex
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Before education, caste or geography, the deepest fault line in who works in India is sex: nearly four in five men are in the workforce, but only two in five women.
chartPlan.howToRead: Compare the two bars: men’s LFPR is 79.2%, women’s 40.3%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not interpret the female rate as a measure of women’s desire to work; it counts unpaid family workers as part of the labour force.

chartPlan.title: Most Indians don't have a job, they have work
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The single most important fact about the shape of Indian work: the typical worker runs a tiny own-account enterprise or helps the family, rather than holding a salaried job.
chartPlan.howToRead: Self-employed (54.5%) includes own-account and unpaid family workers; regular salaried is only 24.7%; casual 20.8%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not conflate ‘self-employed’ with ‘entrepreneur’; most self-employed are survivalist workers.

chartPlan.title: Nine in ten Indians work with no contract and no safety net
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Informality is the water Indian workers swim in: no written contract, no social security, no paid leave, for the overwhelming majority.
chartPlan.howToRead: Overall informality is 85%, rural 90.9%, urban 70.1%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: A lower urban informality rate can mislead; even in cities, 7 in 10 workers are informal.

chartPlan.title: The cruel paradox: the more you study, the more likely you're jobless
chartPlan.whyShowThis: This one chart dissolves the puzzle of low headline unemployment: joblessness is near zero for the unlettered and climbs steeply with every diploma.
chartPlan.howToRead: Not literate UR 0.2%, graduate UR 12.9%, postgraduate 12.3%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Higher unemployment among the educated does not mean education is worthless; it reflects a queue for formal jobs.

chartPlan.title: For the young, a degree is the riskiest qualification of all
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The education paradox is sharpest at the start of working life, where India's graduate-unemployment crisis actually lives.
chartPlan.howToRead: Youth graduate UR 25.6%, postgraduate 32.2%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Youth unemployment rates are higher than all-age rates; the chart focuses on 15–29 year-olds.

chartPlan.title: Of the women who do work, almost a third are paid nothing
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Women's work is not only rarer, it is far more likely to be invisible, unpaid labour on the family farm or in the family shop.
chartPlan.howToRead: 28.8% of working women are unpaid family labour, vs 8.2% of men.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Unpaid family work is economic work, even though no transaction is recorded.

chartPlan.title: Marriage decides whether a woman works, necessity decides harder
chartPlan.whyShowThis: A woman's place in the labour market shifts with her marital status in a way a man's never does.
chartPlan.howToRead: Never-married 24.5%, married 45.3%, widowed 34.7%, divorced/separated 64.6%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume marriage automatically lowers participation; married women often work out of necessity.

chartPlan.title: A woman's faith tracks whether she works
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Religion is another axis along which the female labour market splits, with Muslim women's participation among the lowest.
chartPlan.howToRead: Compare bars: Hindu 41.5%, Muslim 31.4%, Christian 46.7%, etc.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: These are correlations, not causal effects of religion.

chartPlan.title: Same country, a completely different deal for women in every state
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Where a woman is born changes her odds of working more than almost any national policy, the spread across the map is enormous.
chartPlan.howToRead: Choropleth map: darker shades indicate higher female LFPR. Note extreme range from 68.8% to 17.9%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: High female LFPR does not equal high quality of work; many high-FLFP states are poor and agricultural.

chartPlan.title: Who gets handed the hardest, least secure work
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Caste still sorts Indians into kinds of work: the most insecure, day-to-day casual labour falls disproportionately on Dalits and Adivasis.
chartPlan.howToRead: SC casual share 28.6%, ST 26.4%, OBC 20.2%, Others 12.6%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Casual labour is not the only insecure work; self-employed also lack security but are in a different category.

chartPlan.title: ...and who gets the secure salaried jobs
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The mirror image of the casual-work chart: the secure, salaried jobs concentrate at the top of the caste order.
chartPlan.howToRead: Others 33.1% regular salaried, ST only 14.1%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Even regular salaried includes many informal workers; this is share of workers in that status, not necessarily all with contracts.

chartPlan.title: The caste pay gap
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Even among those who do hold a salaried job, what it pays still tracks caste.
chartPlan.howToRead: Median monthly wage: Others ₹18,000, SC ₹12,500, ST ₹13,000.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: These are medians for all salaried workers; occupational differences explain part but not all of the gap.

chartPlan.title: Why measured unemployment is lowest for the most disadvantaged
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Unemployment rates barely differ by caste, and that flatness is itself revealing.
chartPlan.howToRead: UR ST 2.1%, SC 3.3%, OBC 3.2%, Others 3.6%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Low unemployment does not mean good jobs; it reflects the inability to search.

chartPlan.title: Unemployment in India is almost entirely a young person's condition
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The headline jobless rate of under 4% hides the fact that unemployment is overwhelmingly concentrated among the young.
chartPlan.howToRead: Youth (15–29) UR 10.3%, prime-age 0.7%, older 0.1%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: The low prime-age UR does not mean prime-age workers are all in good jobs; many are in low-quality self-employment.

chartPlan.title: The young women who vanish from the data
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The missing-women story has a sharp young edge: a huge share of young women are neither working, studying, nor training.
chartPlan.howToRead: Female NEET 44.3%, male 8.1%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: NEET is not idleness; it often means unpaid domestic work.

chartPlan.title: In the village, you mostly work for yourself
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The rural and urban labour markets are almost different economies; in the countryside, self-employment and casual work dominate.
chartPlan.howToRead: Rural self-employment 60.9%, casual 24.3%, regular only 14.8%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Rural self-employment is not all farming; it includes petty trade, but agriculture is dominant.

chartPlan.title: In the city, you mostly work for a wage
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Cities are where the salaried job lives, about half of urban workers have one, against one in seven in villages.
chartPlan.howToRead: Urban regular salaried 49.7%, self-employment 38.2%, casual 12.1%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Not all urban regular jobs are formal; but the wage-labour relationship is more common.

chartPlan.title: What India's workers actually do all day
chartPlan.whyShowThis: For all the talk of a services superpower, the largest single thing Indian workers do is farm.
chartPlan.howToRead: Agriculture 41.2%, trade/services 23.1%, construction 13%, manufacturing 11.8%, public admin/edu/health 10%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: The low manufacturing share is not a sign of deindustrialisation but of a missing manufacturing boom.

chartPlan.title: Even a salaried job often isn't a 'good' job
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The salaried job is the aspiration, but for half of those who have one, it comes without the protections that are supposed to define it.
chartPlan.howToRead: No contract 55.8%, no social security 53.4%, no paid leave 47.6%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: These percentages are of salaried workers, not all workers; they highlight that salaried status is not equal to formal employment.

chartPlan.title: What a month of work pays, and who gets which deal
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Pay is where every divide in this story compounds: formal vs informal, men vs women.
chartPlan.howToRead: Formal salaried median ₹25,456, informal ₹12,000; men ₹15,500, women ₹10,000.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Casual daily wage (₹400) cannot be directly compared to monthly salaries; a casual worker may not work every day.

chartPlan.title: The poorer the household, the more precarious the work
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Informality and poverty reinforce each other: bad work keeps households poor, and poor households can only get bad work.
chartPlan.howToRead: Informality rate falls from 92.8% for the poorest quartile to 71.4% for the richest.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Even the richest quartile has 7 in 10 workers informal; formality is a thin sliver at the top.

chartPlan.title: Stack the advantages, and this is the gap
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Every divide in this story is a single cut; this is what happens when you stack them. It is the whole argument in one chart.
chartPlan.howToRead: From 38.9% (urban upper-caste male graduate) down to 0.3% (rural Dalit/Adivasi low-educated woman).
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: The profiles are illustrative; the gap is not an exact prediction but a demonstration of intersectional inequality.

visualizationDecisions.indicatorOrVisual: Whether you work at all comes down, first, to your sex
visualizationDecisions.reason: A simple bar chart with two values clearly shows the sex gap in labour force participation for a single year.

visualizationDecisions.indicatorOrVisual: Most Indians don't have a job, they have work
visualizationDecisions.reason: A bar chart of the composition of employment status is intuitive and allows direct comparison.

visualizationDecisions.indicatorOrVisual: Nine in ten Indians work with no contract and no safety net
visualizationDecisions.reason: Comparing overall, rural, and urban informality rates with bars shows the gradient while keeping the chart simple.

visualizationDecisions.indicatorOrVisual: The cruel paradox: the more you study, the more likely you're jobless
visualizationDecisions.reason: A bar chart ordered by education level makes the rising trend immediately visible.

visualizationDecisions.indicatorOrVisual: For the young, a degree is the riskiest qualification of all
visualizationDecisions.reason: Same as education paradox but restricted to 15–29; bars keep the comparison consistent.

visualizationDecisions.indicatorOrVisual: Same country, a completely different deal for women in every state
visualizationDecisions.reason: A map is the most effective way to show wide geographical variation at a glance.

visualizationDecisions.indicatorOrVisual: Stack the advantages, and this is the gap
visualizationDecisions.reason: A small-multiples or grouped bar chart showing the five illustrative profiles works well to highlight the cumulative inequality.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Suppose you gather 100 adults aged 15 and above. LFPR tells you how many of those 100 are either working or actively looking for work. It is not about whether you want a job, only whether you are in the game. In India, that often means counting unpaid help on the family farm as 'work'.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: LFPR is the most basic measure of who is economically active. The entire article is built around LFPR differences by sex, caste, religion, and state.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Of every 100 adults aged 15 and above, WPR is the number who have any kind of work, paid or unpaid. It excludes those who are looking but haven’t found work. Because in India hardly anyone can stay openly jobless for long, WPR is often a more reliable picture of actual work than the unemployment rate.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: The gender gap in WPR is stark, and unpaid work inflates female WPR. We use both LFPR and WPR to show the full picture.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: UR counts only those who are actively seeking work but haven’t found it, as a share of the labour force (the sum of employed and unemployed). It does not count people who have stopped looking. A low UR can mean everyone is working or that no one can afford to search.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: The main puzzle of the Indian labour market is that UR is low (around 3%) while job quality is poor. This concept explains why the educated have higher UR and the poor have lower UR.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: A worker is informal if they have no written contract, no social security (like PF or health insurance), and no paid leave. You can be a salaried employee in an office but still informal if your employer gives you cash and no benefits. It is not about small firms, it is about the absence of a legal employment relationship.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Nine in ten Indian workers are informal. The article shows that even among salaried jobs, a majority lack these protections.

glossaryPlan.term: NEET (Not in Employment, Education or Training)
glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: NEET captures young people aged 15–29 who are neither working, studying, nor in any vocational training. For young women, being NEET overwhelmingly means being engaged in unpaid domestic work like cooking, cleaning, and caregiving, work that is essential but invisible in labour statistics.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: The female NEET rate of 44.3% is one of the most striking numbers in the article. It tells the story of missing women in the workforce and the scale of unrecognised work.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: A secure formal job, as used in the compounding chart, means a regular salaried position that also comes with social security (like provident fund or health insurance). It is the gold standard of employment in India, stable, with some safety net, and the article shows how out of reach it is for many.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: The entire story of inequality culminates in the share of people who hold a secure formal job. The compound chart shows that this share plummets from 38.9% to 0.3% as disadvantages stack.

proseRules: No calculations unless the evidence packet supplies the derived number

plannedCharts.title: Whether you work at all comes down, first, to your sex
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · labour-force participation by sex
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Before education, caste or geography, the deepest fault line in who works in India is sex: nearly four in five men are in the workforce, but only two in five women.

plannedCharts.title: Most Indians don't have a job, they have work
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · workers by status in employment
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The single most important fact about the shape of Indian work: the typical worker runs a tiny own-account enterprise or helps the family, rather than holding a salaried job.

plannedCharts.title: Nine in ten Indians work with no contract and no safety net
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · informal employment, overall and by location
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Informality is the water Indian workers swim in: no written contract, no social security, no paid leave, for the overwhelming majority.

plannedCharts.title: The cruel paradox: the more you study, the more likely you're jobless
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · unemployment rate by education level
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: This one chart dissolves the puzzle of low headline unemployment: joblessness is near zero for the unlettered and climbs steeply with every diploma.

plannedCharts.title: For the young, a degree is the riskiest qualification of all
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · youth unemployment by education level
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The education paradox is sharpest at the start of working life, where India's graduate-unemployment crisis actually lives.

plannedCharts.title: Of the women who do work, almost a third are paid nothing
plannedCharts.unit: % of that sex's workers who are unpaid family labour
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · unpaid family workers, by sex
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Women's work is not only rarer, it is far more likely to be invisible, unpaid labour on the family farm or in the family shop.

plannedCharts.title: Marriage decides whether a woman works, necessity decides harder
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · female participation by marital status
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: A woman's place in the labour market shifts with her marital status in a way a man's never does.

plannedCharts.title: A woman's faith tracks whether she works
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · female participation by religion
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Religion is another axis along which the female labour market splits, with Muslim women's participation among the lowest.

plannedCharts.title: Same country, a completely different deal for women in every state
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · female labour-force participation (ps+ss, 15+) by state
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Where a woman is born changes her odds of working more than almost any national policy, the spread across the map is enormous.

plannedCharts.title: Who gets handed the hardest, least secure work
plannedCharts.unit: % of that group's workers in casual labour
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · casual-labour share by social group
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Caste still sorts Indians into kinds of work: the most insecure, day-to-day casual labour falls disproportionately on Dalits and Adivasis.

plannedCharts.title: ...and who gets the secure salaried jobs
plannedCharts.unit: % of that group's workers in regular salaried jobs
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · regular-salaried share by social group
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The mirror image of the casual-work chart: the secure, salaried jobs concentrate at the top of the caste order.

plannedCharts.title: The caste pay gap
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · median monthly salaried earnings by social group
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Even among those who do hold a salaried job, what it pays still tracks caste.

plannedCharts.title: Why measured unemployment is lowest for the most disadvantaged
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · unemployment rate by social group
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Unemployment rates barely differ by caste, and that flatness is itself revealing.

plannedCharts.title: Unemployment in India is almost entirely a young person's condition
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · unemployment rate by age band
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The headline jobless rate of under 4% hides the fact that unemployment is overwhelmingly concentrated among the young.

plannedCharts.title: The young women who vanish from the data
plannedCharts.unit: % of youth 15–29 not in employment, education or training
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · NEET rate by sex
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The missing-women story has a sharp young edge: a huge share of young women are neither working, studying, nor training.

plannedCharts.title: In the village, you mostly work for yourself
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · rural workers by status
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The rural and urban labour markets are almost different economies; in the countryside, self-employment and casual work dominate.

plannedCharts.title: In the city, you mostly work for a wage
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · urban workers by status
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Cities are where the salaried job lives, about half of urban workers have one, against one in seven in villages.

plannedCharts.title: What India's workers actually do all day
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · workers by broad industry
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: For all the talk of a services superpower, the largest single thing Indian workers do is farm.

plannedCharts.title: Even a salaried job often isn't a 'good' job
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · regular-salaried workers lacking each protection
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The salaried job is the aspiration, but for half of those who have one, it comes without the protections that are supposed to define it.

plannedCharts.title: What a month of work pays, and who gets which deal
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · median monthly salaried earnings
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Pay is where every divide in this story compounds: formal vs informal, men vs women.

plannedCharts.title: The poorer the household, the more precarious the work
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · informality by household consumption quartile
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Informality and poverty reinforce each other: bad work keeps households poor, and poor households can only get bad work.

plannedCharts.title: Stack the advantages, and this is the gap
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · share with a regular salaried job + social security, by stacked identity
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Every divide in this story is a single cut; this is what happens when you stack them. It is the whole argument in one chart.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
